children.title: Polar ordering of linear rod-like polyamide with different linking structure of nonlinear optical chromophores
children.metadata: Nonlinear Optics; ETH Zurich; Ajou University; Molecular Science; Optical Research
children.content:
O-Pil Kwon a,* Daniele Rezzonico a, Seong-Ji Kwon b, Moja Jazbinsk a, Axelle Tappomnier a, Peter Gunter a, Suck-Hyun Lee b<|txt_split|>a Nonlinear Optics Laboratory, ETH Zurich, CH-8093 Zurich, Switzerland  
b Department of Molecular Science and Technology, Ajow University, Sunow 442-749, Republic of Korea<|txt_split|>Received 14 September 2005; accepted 12 January 2006  
Available online 6 March 2006

children.title: Abstract
children.metadata: Nonlinear optical polymer; Polyamide; Second harmonic generation; Chromophore structure; Helical conformation; Internal stress; Corona poling
children.content: We have investigated the correlation between the macroscopic physical properties and the microscopic molecular structure for linear rod-like polyamides with different linking structure of nonlinear optical chromophores by in situ corona poling and optical second harmonic generation. These polymers consist of a wholly aromatic rigid backbone poly( \(p\) -phenylene terephthalamide), PPTA and nonlinear optical chromophores of various sizes. Though all polymers with different linking structure of chromophores possess similar \(\alpha\) - and \(\beta\) -helices, the polymerization process is highly complex. The main challenge is that the partially polyamide F-DVCVA with a flexibly linked bulky chromophore exhibits a high internal stress due to a thermal contraction of the rigid polyamide backbone, which at room temperature can overcome the orientational force of the externally applied electric field.<|txt_split|>\(\text{念}\) 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.<|txt_split|>PACS:42.65.-k<|txt_split|>Keywords: Nonlinear optical polymer, Polyamide; Second harmonic generation

subnode.title: Abstract_1
subnode.metadata: polyamides; chromophore linking; α-β helices; internal stress; corona poling; second harmonic generation
subnode.content: We have investigated the correlation between the macroscopic physical properties and the microscopic molecular structure for linear rod-like polyamides with different linking structure of nonlinear optical chromophores by in situ corona poling and optical second harmonic generation. These polymers consist of a wholly aromatic rigid backbone poly( \(p\) -phenylene terephthalamide), PPTA and nonlinear optical chromophores of various sizes. Though all polymers with different linking structure of chromophores possess similar \(\alpha\) - and \(\beta\) -helices, the polymerization process is highly complex. The main challenge is that the partially polyamide F-DVCVA with a flexibly linked bulky chromophore exhibits a high internal stress due to a thermal contraction of the rigid polyamide backbone, which at room temperature can overcome the orientational force of the externally applied electric field.

subnode.title: Abstract_2
subnode.metadata: Nonlinear optical polymer; Polyamide; Second harmonic generation
subnode.content: \(\text{念}\) 2006 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.PACS:42.65.-kKeywords: Nonlinear optical polymer, Polyamide; Second harmonic generation

children.title: 1. Introduction
children.metadata: hairy-rod polymers; aromatic rigid backbone; NLO chromophores; polyamides; solubility and processability; π-π interactions; steric hindrance; poling dynamics; mesophase behavior; thermal stress; chromophore linking structure
children.content: Hairy-rod polymers based on a wholly aromatic rigid backbone, polyester and polyamide with flexible sidechains have been studied extensively for the last two decades. The main goal of this study was to investigate having better processability and greater solubility [1-5]. The correlation between the microscopic chemical structure and the macroscopic physical properties for the hairy-rod-like polymers with bulky functionalized side groups such as nonlinear optical (NLO) active chromophores [6], photoconducting groups [7], and other functionalized side groups [8] has been investigated. The present group have barely been considered in literature up to<|txt_contd|>now. The molecular design of polymeric functional materials should be related to a macroscopic phase behavior to anticipate the possible physical responses. However, the molecular design of polymeric functional materials is dependent on the variation of their physical properties because of their intricate interactions, such as \(\pi-\pi\) interaction, steric hindrance, rotational barrier. Additionally, the macroscopic response is much more complicated if the polymer has stiff elements and chain—chain, e.g. the NLO chromophores, and the main-chain.<|txt_split|>We have recently reported the NLO properties of sidechain polymers based on a rigid backbone, modified poly(phenylene) and poly(phenylene) (PPPTA) chains, and poly \((p\)-phenylene terephthalamide), PPTA [14,15] The poled polymer films with wholly aromatic rigid-rod backbone (e.g. PPT and PPTA) exhibit a unique mounthing behavior, which is consistent with the structure of NLO polymers [11,14]. We have observed a considerably<|txt_contd|>different mesophase behavior depending on the linking structure of the chromophores, when similar NLO chromophores are used. The results show that the two groups attached to the orienting polymeric backbone [6]. Moreover, the macroscopic response such as micropatterned domain structures and poling dynamics also depended on the mode of the linking structure and the orientation of the groups attached to the orienting polymeric backbone [13].<|txt_split|>In this paper, we investigate the polar ordering process for a series of the linear rod-like stiff polyamides (PPTA). The in situ corona poling and second harmonic generation (SHG) measurements were performed to obtain a better understanding of the molecular structure of the NLO chromophore chemical structure and the macroscopic physical properties. The investigated materials had different linking structures between the rigid backbone and the NLO chromophore while keeping the same backbone poly(p-phenylene terephthalamide), PPTA of the linear rod-shape. Their macroscopic response strongly depends on small differences in the bending structure. Especially the polymer F-DCVA with a flexible linking structure of the bulky chromophore is worthy of notice, in which a strong internal thermal stress could overcome the effect of the externally applied electric field.

subnode.title: 1. Introduction_1
subnode.metadata: Hairy-rod polymers; aromatic rigid backbone; flexible sidechains; processability; solubility; NLO chromophores; photoconducting groups; functionalized side groups
subnode.content: Hairy-rod polymers based on a wholly aromatic rigid backbone, polyester and polyamide with flexible sidechains have been studied extensively for the last two decades. The main goal of this study was to investigate having better processability and greater solubility [1-5]. The correlation between the microscopic chemical structure and the macroscopic physical properties for the hairy-rod-like polymers with bulky functionalized side groups such as nonlinear optical (NLO) active chromophores [6], photoconducting groups [7], and other functionalized side groups [8] has been investigated. The present group have barely been considered in literature up to

subnode.title: 1. Introduction_2
subnode.metadata: molecular design; macroscopic phase behavior; physical properties; π-π interaction; steric hindrance; rotational barrier; stiff elements; chain–chain interactions; NLO chromophores; main-chain interactions
subnode.content: now. The molecular design of polymeric functional materials should be related to a macroscopic phase behavior to anticipate the possible physical responses. However, the molecular design of polymeric functional materials is dependent on the variation of their physical properties because of their intricate interactions, such as \(\pi-\pi\) interaction, steric hindrance, rotational barrier. Additionally, the macroscopic response is much more complicated if the polymer has stiff elements and chain—chain, e.g. the NLO chromophores, and the main-chain.

subnode.title: 1. Introduction_3
subnode.metadata: NLO properties; rigid-rod backbone; mesophase behavior; chromophore linking structure; micropatterned domains; poling dynamics
subnode.content: We have recently reported the NLO properties of sidechain polymers based on a rigid backbone, modified poly(phenylene) and poly(phenylene) (PPPTA) chains, and poly \((p\)-phenylene terephthalamide), PPTA [14,15] The poled polymer films with wholly aromatic rigid-rod backbone (e.g. PPT and PPTA) exhibit a unique mounthing behavior, which is consistent with the structure of NLO polymers [11,14]. We have observed a considerablydifferent mesophase behavior depending on the linking structure of the chromophores, when similar NLO chromophores are used. The results show that the two groups attached to the orienting polymeric backbone [6]. Moreover, the macroscopic response such as micropatterned domain structures and poling dynamics also depended on the mode of the linking structure and the orientation of the groups attached to the orienting polymeric backbone [13].

subnode.title: 1. Introduction_4
subnode.metadata: PPTA polymer; polar ordering; in situ corona poling; SHG measurements; NLO chromophore; linking structure; thermal stress; electric field effect; rod-like stiff polyamides; macroscopic response
subnode.content: In this paper, we investigate the polar ordering process for a series of the linear rod-like stiff polyamides (PPTA). The in situ corona poling and second harmonic generation (SHG) measurements were performed to obtain a better understanding of the molecular structure of the NLO chromophore chemical structure and the macroscopic physical properties. The investigated materials had different linking structures between the rigid backbone and the NLO chromophore while keeping the same backbone poly(p-phenylene terephthalamide), PPTA of the linear rod-shape. Their macroscopic response strongly depends on small differences in the bending structure. Especially the polymer F-DCVA with a flexible linking structure of the bulky chromophore is worthy of notice, in which a strong internal thermal stress could overcome the effect of the externally applied electric field.

children.title: 2.1.Materials
children.metadata: wholly aromatic polymers; PPTA structure; linear rod-shape; para-position diacid/diamine; flexible methylene spacers; NLO chromophores; dicyanovinylation; tricyanovinylation; azo coupling; solution condensation; postfunctionalization; purification; alkyl chain length; NLO response; rigid polyester comparison
children.content: The polymers used here are based on a wholly aromatic structure, with the main chain being \(10\mathrm{mm}\) long (main chain is made of methyl), PPTA which has a linear rod-shape resulting from<|txt_contd|>the para-position of diacid groups in the terephthalate moiety, and the para-position of diamine groups in the \(p\)-phenylenediamine (see Scheme 1). The NLO chromophores are attached with different linking structures via flexible methylene spacers. The linking structures are either flexible consisting of a conventional amine \(N\)-ethylaminoethanol or a bridged \((3^{\prime}\) -propano[4,5-d]pyrrolidinone) group [26]. For example, the structure of the bisolee connection and \(\mathbb{R}^{*}\) stands for the attachment of the bisolee connection and \(\mathbb{R}^{*}\) stands for the rigid connection. The precursor monomers, 2,5-disubstituted terephthalic acid without NLO functionality were usually postfunctionalized by dicyanovinylation, tricyanovinylation, and azo coupling reaction to form the NLO chromophores. The polymers were prepared by the direct solution condensation of the NLO functionalized terephthalic acid with \(p\)-phenylenediamine. The details of the syntheses of the monomers and polymers were given in Table 1. The resulting products were purified by using four methylene units \((n = 4)\) was chosen here for the reason that the changes of the length of the alkyl chain resulted in large changes of the NLO response and the polymers with shorter spacers exhibited generally better NLO properties in the previously studied rigid polyester [12].

children.title: 2.2 Thin film preparation
children.metadata: Polymer film preparation; NMP dissolution; Teflon filtration; water mixing; 1000-4000 nm filter; spinning at 1000-4000 rpm; vacuum drying at 50°C; 24h drying; 1.5μm thickness; single-step preflow etcher; AFM characterization
children.content: Polymer films for nonlinear optical measurement were prepared by dissolution of the polymers in \(N\)-methylpyrrolidone-2-one (NMP), followed by filtration through a \(0.45\mu \mathrm{m}\) Teflon membrane filter. The solutions were then mixed with an appropriate amount of water and filtered using a \(1000 - 4000\mathrm{nm}\) filter, with spinning rate in the range \(1000 - 4000\mathrm{rpm}\). After spinning, the samples were dried in vacuum with final pressure<|txt_contd|>of less than \(0.5\mathrm{mbar}\) at \(50^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for at least \(24\mathrm{h}\). The thickness of the film is \(1.5\mu \mathrm{m}\) and the film is a single-step preflow etcher and atomic force microscopy (AFM).

children.title: 日： Scheme 1. Chemical structures of the investigated polymers consist of a wholly aromatic rigid backbone poly( \(p\cdot \) -phenylene terephthalamide), PPTA and different linking structure of nonlinear optical chromophores of various sizes.
children.metadata: polymer, chemical structure, aromatic backbone, polyphenylene terephthalamide, nonlinear optical chromophores
children.content: The image displays a chemical structure diagram labeled as "Scheme 1," illustrating the molecular structure of a polymer composed of a bulky aromatic rigid backbone known as poly(p-phenylene terephthalamide) (PPTA). The structure features a repeating unit with a central benzene ring connected to a carbonyl group and an amide linkage, which is further linked to a phenyl ring substituted with a non-linear optical chromophore group (labeled as R), and another phenyl ring with an amine group. The structure is shown as a repeating unit, indicating the polymer chain, and the chromophore group is labeled with the formula "OCH₂H₂R" and "R" for variable substitution. The overall structure emphasizes the incorporation of nonlinear optical chromophores into the polymer backbone.

children.title: R-NPP
children.metadata: Chemical structure, R-NPP, nitrogen-containing aromatic compound
children.content: The image displays the chemical structure of a compound labeled as "R-NPP". It consists of a nitrogen atom bonded to a five-membered ring (pyrrolidine) and a benzene ring substituted with a nitro group (-NO₂). A methylene group (-CH₂-) connects the pyrrolidine ring to an ether oxygen, which is further attached to a carbon bearing the "R" label, indicating a variable substituent. The structure is presented in a clear, schematic format typical of organic chemistry diagrams.

children.title: F-DCV
children.metadata: Chemical structure; F-DCV; organic compound; molecular diagram
children.content: The image displays the chemical structure of F-DCV, an organic compound. It features a central benzene ring with a cyano group (–NC) attached to one carbon and a vinyl group (–CH=CH2) attached to another. A nitrogen atom bonded to the benzene ring is connected to a chain consisting of an ethyl group (–CH2CH3) and an ethoxy group (–OCH2CH3). The molecule is labeled "F-DCV" below the structure.

children.title: F-NPA
children.metadata: Chemical structure; F-NPA; aromatic amine; diaminobenzene derivative
children.content: The image displays the chemical structure of a compound labeled "F-NPA," which consists of a central benzene ring connected via an amine linkage to another benzene ring bearing a nitro group (–NO₂) at its para position. The first benzene ring is further connected to a side chain containing an ethyl group and a terminal –OCH₂CH₂– group, forming a branched alkyl-oxygen structure. The entire molecule exhibits a linear arrangement with two aromatic rings and a functionalized alkyl side chain.

children.title: R-DCV
children.metadata: Chemical structure, R-DCV, organic molecule, bicyclic ring, benzene, nitrile, amine
children.content: The image displays the chemical structure of R-DCV, an organic compound featuring a bicyclic ring system connected via a nitrogen atom to a benzene ring. The benzene ring is further linked to a vinyl group containing a nitrile (NC) substituent and an amino (NH) group. A methoxy (OCH3) group is attached to the bicyclic ring. The molecule is labeled as "R-DCV" at the bottom.

children.title: F-TCV
children.metadata: Chemical structure; F-TCV; organic molecule; cyanide groups; benzene ring; ethylamine; ether linkage
children.content: The image displays the chemical structure of a compound labeled "F-TCV". It features a benzene ring with substituents: one side has a cyanide group (–NC) attached to a carbon atom bonded to a cyanide group (–CN), while the other side has a nitrogen atom bonded to an ethyl group (–CH2CH3) and an ethyl ether group (–CH2CH2OCH3). The molecule includes multiple cyanide functionalities and a nitrogen-containing side chain.

children.title: F-DCVA
children.metadata: Chemical structure; F-DCVA; organic molecule; benzene rings; amine; nitrile; ethoxy group
children.content: The image displays the chemical structure of F-DCVA, an organic compound featuring a central linker connecting two benzene rings via amine groups. The left benzene ring is attached to a branched ethoxy group (–OCH2CH3) and an ethylamine group (–NHCH2CH3). The right benzene ring is connected to a nitrile group (–C≡N) and a methyl group (–CH3), forming a substituted benzene ring. The entire structure is labeled "F-DCVA" beneath the diagram.

children.title: 2.3. In situ corona poling and second harmonic generation
children.metadata: corona discharge; spin-coated films; SHG measurements; Q-switched pump laser; poling; p-polarized light; Y-cut quartz reference; Maker fringe technique
children.content: The spin-coated films were subjected to a corona discharge using a positive voltage of \(5\mathrm{kV}\) applied to the tip of a tungsten needle placed \(10\mathrm{mm}\) away from the film surface. SHG measurements were made using a Q-switched pump laser with a wavelength of \(400\mathrm{nm}\) and a repetition rate of \(10\mathrm{Hz}\). In order to perform simultaneously the poling and SHG measurement, the p-polarized light was incident at an angle of about \(40^{\circ}\) to the film surface with an external electric field and various poling temperatures. A reference signal of Y-cut quartz plate was monitored continuously. The results showed that under fluctuations and correct for possible drifts in the laser intensity level. The second-order NLO susceptibilities were measured by a conventional Maker fringe technique [16].

children.title: 3.1. Thermal properties
children.metadata: glass transition temperature; rigid side-chains; flexible backbones; α-transition; β-transition; DSC measurement; thermotropic mesophases; dipole moment; aromatic side groups; backbone motion; side-chain motion; weak broad peaks; microscopic chemical structure; physical properties; heat flow changes
children.content: The glass transition temperatures of the investigated polymers measured by differential scanning calorimeter (DSC, scan rate \(10\mathrm{K / min}\)) are listed in Table 1. Phase transitions of the studied polymers were observed for both rigid side-chains are complex rather, while the polymers with flexible backbones usually reveal only one glass transition temperature \(T_{p}\). In the case of the rigid backbone polymers that we were investigating, the glass transition temperature was found to be about \(25^{\circ}C\) and the transition conditions while the motion of side-chains tended to separate from the motion of main-chains [17]. The higher transition temperature is associated with the rigid main-chain motions (\(\alpha\)-transition, \(T_{\alpha}\)) and the lower transition temperature is associated with side-chain motions (\(\beta\)-transition, \(T_{\beta}\)). The transition temperature was found to be at the starting point of the broad endothermic transition. These polymers did not show any peaks of thermotropic mesophases, either with the flexible connection or the rigid connection. In general, the glass transition temperature of these polymers was found to be between \(300^{\circ}C\) and \(400^{\circ}C\) and the dipole moment of the side groups [18]. However, the lower and the higher glass transitions in all inves<|txt_contd|>tigated polymers are both observable as weak broad peaks at approximately similar temperature ranges, around \(150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for the \(\alpha\)-transition characteristic of the polymer backbone and around \(85^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for the \(\beta\)-transition characteristic of the polymer backbone. The transition temperatures of the highly stiff polyamide of linear rod-shape seem to be independent of the microscopic chemical structure of the side groups such as size, aromaticity, rigidity, and moreover, the linking structure. The glass transition behavior of either the side-chain \((\beta\)-transition, \(T_{B})\) or the side-chain \((\gamma\)-transition, \(T_{\gamma})\) is also observed in all polymers; for all polymers; in most cases, the DSC chemodiamids exhibit a changing slope only, without a visible peak and without a clear ending temperature of the transitions. The changes of the heat flow for the glass transitions are more than one order of magnitude smaller than first-order fluctuations of the side chain. In addition, the differences of the bulky aromatic side groups with small thermal fluctuations of the stiff backbone. Therefore we could not clearly distinguish the difference in both glass transition temperatures \((T_{\alpha}, T_{\beta})\) resulting from small differences of the microscopic chemical structures. However, their physical properties, which are presented in the following, show that these differences even if the differently linked polymers do not show considerable variations of glass transition temperatures.

children.title: Table 1 Summary of the properties of the polymers used in this work. B-glass transition temperatures \(T_{\mathrm{B}}\) associated with side-chain motions, \(\alpha\)-glass transition temperature \(T_{\mathrm{a}}\) associated with main-chain motions, and the effective thickness-normalized second-order NLO susceptibility \(d_{\mathrm{A}}\) as defined in the text

children.title: 3.2. Contribution of rigid main-chain to polar ordering
children.metadata: spin-coated films; corona poling; nonlinear optical susceptibility; SHG measurement; chromophore structure; glass transition dynamics; flexible vs rigid linking; mesophase behavior; side-chain motion; main-chain coupling; poling temperature effect; relaxation dynamics; NLO coefficient; polymer backbone; thermal fluctuations; alignment stability; chromophore size effect
children.content: The spin-coated films were corona-poled under a voltage of \(5\mathrm{kV}\) in air in order to induce the noncentrosymmetric polar order of the NLO chromophores. Then, the nonlinearity was measured using a conventional Maker fringe technique. Since quantitative information on the second-order NLO susceptibility is difficult to obtain from the film, it is in a similar series of polyesters [12], the effective second-order NLO susceptibility \((d_{\mathrm{eff}})\) defined as a thickness-normalized SHG intensity, was compared for the polymers with different chromophores. It is given by \(d_{\mathrm{eff}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\rho_{\mathrm{melt}} / q_{\mathrm{surf}} / \ell}\), where \(I\) is the SHG intensity of light generated by the polymer or the reference quartz plate (1 mm thick), and \(\ell\) is the thickness of the spin-coated film. For the nonlinear optical susceptibility of quartz at \(1000\mathrm{nm}\) is calculated by the following equation of change with Ref. [19]. The values of thickness-normalized effective NLO susceptibility \(d_{\mathrm{eff}}\) after poling are listed in Table 1. These results, especially, showed that polymers<|txt_contd|>with chromophores of larger nonlinearity exhibit higher NLO coefficient in the same way like general flexible NLO polymers, except for the F-DCVA polymer which will be discussed later.<|txt_split|>Although the series of the investigated polymers show similar glass transition temperatures and follow a general trend of the effective NLO susceptibility \(d_{\mathrm{eff}}\), the dynamics of the transition temperature is highly complex. The main focus on the linking structure of the chromophores, Fig. 1 shows the results of SHG experiments by the in situ measurement for two polymers with the same polymer backbone and the same chromophore (i.e. same size and same interaction) (Fig. 2). In addition to the experimental conditions are the following: first, the samples are heated near to the \(\beta\)-transition temperature \(T_{\beta}\) (\(\approx 100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\)), and then the poling electric field is applied. After stabilization of the initial growth of the SHG signal, the temperature is increased near the \(\alpha\)-glass transition region. This is due to the increase in the number of molecules close to room temperature. Comparison of the initial transients shows that increase of the SHG intensity in both samples is similar, but their behavior near the high temperature region related to the \(\alpha\)-transition temperature \(T_{\alpha}\) is different. The reason for this difference is that the presence of an electronic connection increases and stabilizes, while for the F-DCV polymer with the flexible connection it decays when<|txt_contd|>increasing the temperature to the \(\alpha\)-glass transition region. In the case of the flexibly linked F-DCV polymer the poling field could not overcome the thermal fluctuations of the chromophores in the \(\alpha\)-transition temperature region. The main chain consists of a rigid monolayer with a large number of the main-chain with the higher glass transition temperature \(T_{\mathrm{g}}\) is not strongly correlated with the motion of the side-chain with the lower glass transition temperature \(T_{\mathrm{p}}\) whereas in the case of the R-DCV polymer the side- and transverse directions are parallel to each other. This behavior in the region of the \(\alpha\)-glass transition temperature can be explained by the remained mesophase-structure characteristics resulting from the linear linking. Recently, the wholly aromatic polysters of linear rod-shape have shown a considerably different mesophase behavior [6]: an increase in the side-chain length leads to a strong tropic mesophases, while PPT-NPP with a rigid connection did not exhibit it. According to the previous results, the flexible connection like in the F-DCV polymer is more likely to result in mesophase characteristics than the rigid connection, even if the mesophase transition cannot be completely reversed. However, this phenomenon occurs in the F-DCV polymer is much more separated from the main-chain motion in contrast to the rigid connection polymer (R-DCV), which exhibits more amorphous characteristics. Therefore, the flexible connection leads to a larger free volume and lower rotational barrier than the rigid connection, leading to the coupled motion between the main-and side-chains.<|txt_split|>However, also in the case of the F-DCV polymer with the flexible linking structure, the side-chain motion is not completely uncoupled from the main-chain motion either, i.e. the rigid main-chain slightly affects the side-chain motion. Fig. 2 shows the relaxation dynamics with different side-chain positions for the P-DF (near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\)) and the side-chain motion (near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\) as well as near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\) as shown in Fig. 2a). In the condition B, the electric field was turned on at \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) (\(n = 150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\)) like for the condition A, and then the temperature increased to \(150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) (\(n = 3.95^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\)) like for the condition C. Despite of the large difference of the poling temperatures, similar values of the effective NLO susceptibility \(d_{\mathrm{eff}} = 2.9 \pm 0.3 \mu \mathrm{m}^{-1}\) are found. This is consistent with the alignment of the chain molecules in front of the F-DCV polymer, whereas in the lower glass transition temperature \(T_{\mathrm{g}}\) as shown in Fig. 1. In contrast to the steady-state optical nonlinearity, the relaxation for the condition A with only side-chain motion during poling is faster than that of the poling conditions B and C where also a main-chain motion was involved during poling, as seen in Fig. 2b. For the conditions B and C with both side- and main-chain motions a small change in the side-chain motion occurred. Therefore the motion of the stiff main-chains in the case of F-DCV is still slightly correlated with the side-chain motion.<|txt_split|>We can also clearly observe the influence of the remaining polymer on the thermodynamic properties of the polymeration dynamics for a series of the polymers with different<|txt_contd|>chromophores (see Fig. 3). In the case of the polymers with the rigid connection (R-NPP and R-DCV), the temporal stability clearly increases with increasing the size of the chromophores, whereas the polymers with the flexible con<|txt_contd|>nction (F-TCV and F-NPA) show a similar dynamics despite of the considerably different size of the chromophores. Moreover, the rigidly connected polymers with smaller chromophores exhibit a more stable orientation of the chromophores than that of the flexibly connected polymers with larger chromophores. This means that the main-chain orientation is not as important as to be helpful to effectively prevent the relaxation of the side-chain motion in the polymers with the rigid connection.

children.title: Fig. 1. (a) Poling-temperature sequence; (b) Field-induced growth of the PPTA and the PPTC films; (c) PPTA and PPTC films with different PPTA and the same chemical composition; (d) PPTA and the same chemical composition; (e) DCV with different linking structures; (f) PPTA and the same chemical composition; (g) PPTC with different linking structures.
children.metadata: SHG, corona poling, polymer backbone, PPTA, DCV, F-DCV, temperature, electric field
children.content: The image displays two plots labeled (a) and (b). Plot (a) is a poling-temperature sequence showing temperature (in °C) versus time, with a sharp rise to 120°C, followed by a plateau and then a decline. Key markers include the threshold temperature T_p and the relaxation time τ_a. Plot (b) shows the field-induced growth of the SHG signal for corona poling at 5 kV on the same polymer backbone, with different linking structures for R-DCV (open squares) and F-DCV (filled circles). The signal grows rapidly after the electric field is turned on and then gradually decays.

children.title: STG Intensity vs Time for R-DCV and F-DCV
children.metadata: STG intensity, R-DCV, F-DCV, time, peak, tau
children.content: The image displays two overlapping curves representing STG intensity (in a.u.) versus time (in μs) for R-DCV (square markers) and F-DCV (circle markers). Both curves exhibit a rapid initial rise followed by a plateau phase, with labeled peak intensities Tp and Tα, and time constants τp and τα, indicating distinct temporal dynamics for each condition.

children.title: Fig.2.a) Poling-temperature sequences for the F-DCV films with treatment conditions A (near \(T_{\mathrm{R}}\) dashed line),B near \(T_{\mathrm{f}}\) and \(T_{\mathrm{e}}\) dotted line),Cnear \(T_{\mathrm{r}}\) solid line),and SHG signal decay of the polymer-F-DCV films. b) The results of the experiments for the conditions A (open circles), C (filled circles), C (open squares).
children.metadata: F-DCY films; poling; temperature sequence; SHG signal decay; poling conditions
children.content: The image displays three poling-temperature sequences (A, B, C) for F-DCY films, plotted as temperature (°C) versus time. Sequence A (solid line) shows a temperature ramp from 100°C to 200°C followed by cooling to 0°C, with E-field on during poling and off during cooling. Sequence B (dotted line) involves a similar ramp but with E-field on during cooling. Sequence C (dashed line) shows a temperature ramp to 100°C, then cooling to 0°C, with E-field on during poling and off during cooling. The SHG signal decay of the polymer F-DCY film is shown for different poling conditions: A (open circles), B (open squares), and C (open triangles). The legend distinguishes between the three sequences.

children.title: SHG Intensity vs. Time and Temperature
children.metadata: SHG intensity, temperature, time; phase transition; experimental data
children.content: The image displays a plot of relative SHG intensity (y-axis left) versus time (x-axis), with temperature (y-axis right) shown as a secondary axis. The SHG intensity decreases over time, with distinct regions labeled A, B, and C indicating different phases or transitions. At approximately 30 minutes, the SHG intensity shows a sudden increase, followed by a plateau, while the temperature rises sharply, reaching 100°C. The data points are plotted as black dots with a smooth curve connecting them, illustrating the dynamics of a phase transition process.

children.title: Fig. 3. SHG signal decay of the polymer films for R-NPP (open squares), R-NPP (filled squares), and R-NPP (dashed circles) at \(100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). The temperature was stepped to 50, 80, 100, 120, and \(140^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\).
children.metadata: SHG, polymer films, R-NPP, R-DCV, F-TCV, F-NPA, temperature step, decay curves
children.content: The image presents a graph illustrating the second harmonic generation (SHG) signal decay over time for different polymer films: R-NPP (open squares), R-DCV (filled squares), F-TCV (open circles), and F-NPA (filled circles). The left y-axis shows relative SHG intensity (in arbitrary units), while the right y-axis shows temperature in degrees Celsius. The x-axis represents time in minutes. The temperature was stepped to 50, 80, 100, 120, and 140°C, with each temperature step shown as a distinct curve. The decay curves indicate varying rates of SHG signal reduction across the polymer types and temperature conditions.

children.title: Fig. 4. Corona poling field-induced growth of the SHG signal for F-DCVA polymer. The electric field was applied at time \(t_0 = 1\mathrm{min}\)
children.metadata: SHG, corona poling, electric field, F-DCVA polymer, time-resolved measurement
children.content: The graph illustrates the time-dependent growth of the second harmonic generation (SHG) signal in an F-DCVA polymer under corona poling and an applied electric field. The x-axis represents time in minutes, while the left y-axis shows the relative SHG intensity (a.u.), and the right y-axis shows the temperature (°C). The black curve represents the SHG intensity, which increases sharply after approximately 10 minutes, while the temperature rises steadily over time, indicating the influence of the electric field on the polymer's molecular alignment and thermal response. The data points are plotted as a series of discrete measurements, showing the dynamic evolution of the SHG signal as a function of time.

subnode.title: 3.2. Contribution of rigid main-chain to polar ordering_1
subnode.metadata: spin-coated films; corona poling; noncentrosymmetric polar order; NLO chromophores; Maker fringe technique; effective second-order NLO susceptibility; thickness-normalized SHG intensity; quartz reference; NLO susceptibility measurement
subnode.content: The spin-coated films were corona-poled under a voltage of \(5\mathrm{kV}\) in air in order to induce the noncentrosymmetric polar order of the NLO chromophores. Then, the nonlinearity was measured using a conventional Maker fringe technique. Since quantitative information on the second-order NLO susceptibility is difficult to obtain from the film, it is in a similar series of polyesters [12], the effective second-order NLO susceptibility \((d_{\mathrm{eff}})\) defined as a thickness-normalized SHG intensity, was compared for the polymers with different chromophores. It is given by \(d_{\mathrm{eff}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{4}\rho_{\mathrm{melt}} / q_{\mathrm{surf}} / \ell}\), where \(I\) is the SHG intensity of light generated by the polymer or the reference quartz plate (1 mm thick), and \(\ell\) is the thickness of the spin-coated film. For the nonlinear optical susceptibility of quartz at \(1000\mathrm{nm}\) is calculated by the following equation of change with Ref. [19]. The values of thickness-normalized effective NLO susceptibility \(d_{\mathrm{eff}}\) after poling are listed in Table 1. These results, especially, showed that polymers

subnode.title: 3.2. Contribution of rigid main-chain to polar ordering_2
subnode.metadata: chromophore nonlinearity; NLO susceptibility; glass transition dynamics; SHG experiments; polymer backbone; electronic connection; poling field; α-transition behavior; β-transition temperature
subnode.content: with chromophores of larger nonlinearity exhibit higher NLO coefficient in the same way like general flexible NLO polymers, except for the F-DCVA polymer which will be discussed later.Although the series of the investigated polymers show similar glass transition temperatures and follow a general trend of the effective NLO susceptibility \(d_{\mathrm{eff}}\), the dynamics of the transition temperature is highly complex. The main focus on the linking structure of the chromophores, Fig. 1 shows the results of SHG experiments by the in situ measurement for two polymers with the same polymer backbone and the same chromophore (i.e. same size and same interaction) (Fig. 2). In addition to the experimental conditions are the following: first, the samples are heated near to the \(\beta\)-transition temperature \(T_{\beta}\) (\(\approx 100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\)), and then the poling electric field is applied. After stabilization of the initial growth of the SHG signal, the temperature is increased near the \(\alpha\)-glass transition region. This is due to the increase in the number of molecules close to room temperature. Comparison of the initial transients shows that increase of the SHG intensity in both samples is similar, but their behavior near the high temperature region related to the \(\alpha\)-transition temperature \(T_{\alpha}\) is different. The reason for this difference is that the presence of an electronic connection increases and stabilizes, while for the F-DCV polymer with the flexible connection it decays when

subnode.title: 3.2. Contribution of rigid main-chain to polar ordering_3
subnode.metadata: flexible linkage; α-glass transition; mesophase behavior; side-chain motion; main-chain rigidity; rotational barrier; free volume; coupled motion; linear rod-shape polymer; tropic mesophases
subnode.content: increasing the temperature to the \(\alpha\)-glass transition region. In the case of the flexibly linked F-DCV polymer the poling field could not overcome the thermal fluctuations of the chromophores in the \(\alpha\)-transition temperature region. The main chain consists of a rigid monolayer with a large number of the main-chain with the higher glass transition temperature \(T_{\mathrm{g}}\) is not strongly correlated with the motion of the side-chain with the lower glass transition temperature \(T_{\mathrm{p}}\) whereas in the case of the R-DCV polymer the side- and transverse directions are parallel to each other. This behavior in the region of the \(\alpha\)-glass transition temperature can be explained by the remained mesophase-structure characteristics resulting from the linear linking. Recently, the wholly aromatic polysters of linear rod-shape have shown a considerably different mesophase behavior [6]: an increase in the side-chain length leads to a strong tropic mesophases, while PPT-NPP with a rigid connection did not exhibit it. According to the previous results, the flexible connection like in the F-DCV polymer is more likely to result in mesophase characteristics than the rigid connection, even if the mesophase transition cannot be completely reversed. However, this phenomenon occurs in the F-DCV polymer is much more separated from the main-chain motion in contrast to the rigid connection polymer (R-DCV), which exhibits more amorphous characteristics. Therefore, the flexible connection leads to a larger free volume and lower rotational barrier than the rigid connection, leading to the coupled motion between the main-and side-chains.

subnode.title: 3.2. Contribution of rigid main-chain to polar ordering_4
subnode.metadata: side-chain motion; main-chain motion; F-DCV polymer; relaxation dynamics; NLO susceptibility; poling temperature; chain alignment; correlated motion; optical nonlinearity
subnode.content: However, also in the case of the F-DCV polymer with the flexible linking structure, the side-chain motion is not completely uncoupled from the main-chain motion either, i.e. the rigid main-chain slightly affects the side-chain motion. Fig. 2 shows the relaxation dynamics with different side-chain positions for the P-DF (near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\)) and the side-chain motion (near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\) as well as near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\) as shown in Fig. 2a). In the condition B, the electric field was turned on at \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) (\(n = 150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\)) like for the condition A, and then the temperature increased to \(150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) (\(n = 3.95^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) near \(T_{\mathrm{d}}\)) like for the condition C. Despite of the large difference of the poling temperatures, similar values of the effective NLO susceptibility \(d_{\mathrm{eff}} = 2.9 \pm 0.3 \mu \mathrm{m}^{-1}\) are found. This is consistent with the alignment of the chain molecules in front of the F-DCV polymer, whereas in the lower glass transition temperature \(T_{\mathrm{g}}\) as shown in Fig. 1. In contrast to the steady-state optical nonlinearity, the relaxation for the condition A with only side-chain motion during poling is faster than that of the poling conditions B and C where also a main-chain motion was involved during poling, as seen in Fig. 2b. For the conditions B and C with both side- and main-chain motions a small change in the side-chain motion occurred. Therefore the motion of the stiff main-chains in the case of F-DCV is still slightly correlated with the side-chain motion.

subnode.title: 3.2. Contribution of rigid main-chain to polar ordering_5
subnode.metadata: rigid connection; chromophore size; temporal stability; side-chain motion; main-chain orientation; polymerization dynamics
subnode.content: We can also clearly observe the influence of the remaining polymer on the thermodynamic properties of the polymeration dynamics for a series of the polymers with differentchromophores (see Fig. 3). In the case of the polymers with the rigid connection (R-NPP and R-DCV), the temporal stability clearly increases with increasing the size of the chromophores, whereas the polymers with the flexible connction (F-TCV and F-NPA) show a similar dynamics despite of the considerably different size of the chromophores. Moreover, the rigidly connected polymers with smaller chromophores exhibit a more stable orientation of the chromophores than that of the flexibly connected polymers with larger chromophores. This means that the main-chain orientation is not as important as to be helpful to effectively prevent the relaxation of the side-chain motion in the polymers with the rigid connection.

children.title: 3.3. Internal stress by thermal contraction of linear rigid polyamide
children.metadata: flexible chromophore; SHG signal decrease; thermal contraction; internal stress; planar orientation; dipole aggregation; rotational barrier; chromophore size; electric field suppression; antiparallel alignment
children.content: The polar ordering process for the F-DCVA polymer with a flexibly linked chromophore of a large size is unusual as shown in Fig. 4 that shows the development of the SHG signal during poling. The temperature was first increased to the \(\beta\)-glass transition temperature \(T_{\beta}\) (\(\sim 100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\)), and then the electric field was applied. The effect of the SHG signal on the temperature distribution and builds up after applying the electric field. However, then the SHG signal unexpectedly decreases to zero during the cooling process. This unexpected behavior also occurs at higher poling temperatures of near or above \(\alpha\)-transition temperature and is reproducible after several cycles of the poling process. This behavior implies that certain internal forces of the F-DCVA polymer compensate the influence of the externally applied electric field during the cooling process, but probably these forces arise due to the internal stress by the thermal contraction of the stiff polyamide main-chains.<|txt_split|>In the case of rigid-rod-like polymers, the film prepared by the spin-coating process leads to a mainly planar orientation of the buckbones by the flow-induced planar stacking. The main chain length of the film is \(100\mathrm{nm}\) and the films are tilted with respect to the main-chain layers to decrease<|txt_contd|>the distance between the neighboring side groups [5]. Therefore, the chromophores can rotate easily in the plane of the film. The restriction forces in the planar oriented backbones do not usually allow a vertical alignment of the chromophores. This is because the chromophores are applied electric field. In the high temperature region the density of the main-chains decreases due to the weaker interactions such as hydrogen bonding, \(\pi\)- interactions of the main-chains, dipole aggregation force to the chromophores. As in a general NLO polymer, the electric field is stronger than the magnetic field and thus the distortion of the parallel arrangement of neighboring chromophores. However, it is still suppressed by the restriction forces mentioned above. Such restricted motion of the bulky chromophores with large microscopic nonlinearities on the F-DCVA polymer lead to macroscopically smaller electronic band gap (EBG) coefficient than that of the polymers with smaller chromophores or lower microscopic nonlinearity (see Table 1).<|txt_split|>The reasons for this unexpected phenomena during the cooling process may be related to four effects. First, the distance between the main-chains decreases as the temperature increases. Second, the thermal transition of the interchain interaction varies as \( r^{\prime \prime} \), where \( r \) is the intermolecular distance. Accordingly, a slight decrease of the interchain distance by the thermal contraction of the planar oriented rigid backbones may induce the compressive force in the film and the deformation of the film. Third, the direction of the external electric field as illustrated in Fig. 5. Thus, the motion of the chromophores in the interior is more restricted. The internal stress assists to induce the planar orientation of the chromophores in the film and antiparallel dipole-dipole aggregation due to the electrostatic interactions between the chromophores. A possible reason is related to the rotational barrier of the chromophore. The rotational energy barrier for the rigid connection is approximately two times higher than that of the flexible connection as described in Ref. [6]. This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that under certain forces. It seems reasonable to expect that the flexible connection is much more favorable to form<|txt_contd|>alignment in the plane compared to the rigid connection. The third suggested reason is the fact that the chromophore DCVA is larger (about \(16\mathrm{A}\)) than the length of the repeating unit \((\approx 13\mathrm{A})\) [6]. In point of view of the structural barrier related to the size of the chromophores, alignment of DCVA molecules is more difficult than for smaller chromophores. This is because the chromophores are not necessarily phenoenoma during the cooling process is that the dipole aggregation progressively increases with increasing the dipole moment caused by more and more electron-withdrawing acceptor groups [21]. Finally this internal stress by the thermal contraction of the main-chains leads a change in the intermolecular configuration from parallel to antiparallel association of the chromophores. According to our previous work, F-DCVA polymer with flexibly linked chromophores of large size, the orientational force of the externally applied electric field could be suppressed at room temperature.

children.title: Fig. 5. Schematic diagram of a planar orientation of polymer backbones by flow-induced planar stacking of the rigid segments for F-DCVA polymer, and chromophores oriented normal to the film due to the influence of the external poling electric field.
children.metadata: polymer, planar orientation, internal stress, chromophore, NLO, PFTA, DCVA
children.content: The image is a schematic diagram illustrating the planar orientation of polymer backbones in an F-DCVA polymer film. It depicts a layered structure with a substrate at the bottom, and a stack of rigid segments of the polymer. The segments are shown with NLO chromophores (indicated as “NLO chromophore, DCVA”) arranged in a linearly linked, alkyl spacer configuration. The inner rod-like polyamide backbone (labeled as “PFTA”) is oriented perpendicularly to the film plane. An arrow labeled “internal stress” points downward, indicating the force driving the planar alignment. The diagram also shows the external polarizing electric field (E_p) acting perpendicular to the film, which is responsible for the alignment of the chromophores. This orientation is critical for achieving high nonlinear optical performance in the material.

subnode.title: 3.3. Internal stress by thermal contraction of linear rigid polyamide_1
subnode.metadata: unusual polar ordering; SHG signal decrease; thermal contraction; internal stress; electric field compensation; α-transition temperature; reproducible poling cycles
subnode.content: The polar ordering process for the F-DCVA polymer with a flexibly linked chromophore of a large size is unusual as shown in Fig. 4 that shows the development of the SHG signal during poling. The temperature was first increased to the \(\beta\)-glass transition temperature \(T_{\beta}\) (\(\sim 100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\)), and then the electric field was applied. The effect of the SHG signal on the temperature distribution and builds up after applying the electric field. However, then the SHG signal unexpectedly decreases to zero during the cooling process. This unexpected behavior also occurs at higher poling temperatures of near or above \(\alpha\)-transition temperature and is reproducible after several cycles of the poling process. This behavior implies that certain internal forces of the F-DCVA polymer compensate the influence of the externally applied electric field during the cooling process, but probably these forces arise due to the internal stress by the thermal contraction of the stiff polyamide main-chains.

subnode.title: 3.3. Internal stress by thermal contraction of linear rigid polyamide_2
subnode.metadata: planar orientation; rigid-rod-like polymers; spin-coating; chromophore rotation; restriction forces; high temperature; NLO polymer; F-DCVA polymer; electronic band gap coefficient
subnode.content: In the case of rigid-rod-like polymers, the film prepared by the spin-coating process leads to a mainly planar orientation of the buckbones by the flow-induced planar stacking. The main chain length of the film is \(100\mathrm{nm}\) and the films are tilted with respect to the main-chain layers to decreasethe distance between the neighboring side groups [5]. Therefore, the chromophores can rotate easily in the plane of the film. The restriction forces in the planar oriented backbones do not usually allow a vertical alignment of the chromophores. This is because the chromophores are applied electric field. In the high temperature region the density of the main-chains decreases due to the weaker interactions such as hydrogen bonding, \(\pi\)- interactions of the main-chains, dipole aggregation force to the chromophores. As in a general NLO polymer, the electric field is stronger than the magnetic field and thus the distortion of the parallel arrangement of neighboring chromophores. However, it is still suppressed by the restriction forces mentioned above. Such restricted motion of the bulky chromophores with large microscopic nonlinearities on the F-DCVA polymer lead to macroscopically smaller electronic band gap (EBG) coefficient than that of the polymers with smaller chromophores or lower microscopic nonlinearity (see Table 1).

subnode.title: 3.3. Internal stress by thermal contraction of linear rigid polyamide_3
subnode.metadata: thermal contraction; interchain distance; compressive force; chromophore orientation; electric field restriction; dipole-dipole aggregation; rotational barrier; flexible vs rigid connection
subnode.content: The reasons for this unexpected phenomena during the cooling process may be related to four effects. First, the distance between the main-chains decreases as the temperature increases. Second, the thermal transition of the interchain interaction varies as \( r^{\prime \prime} \), where \( r \) is the intermolecular distance. Accordingly, a slight decrease of the interchain distance by the thermal contraction of the planar oriented rigid backbones may induce the compressive force in the film and the deformation of the film. Third, the direction of the external electric field as illustrated in Fig. 5. Thus, the motion of the chromophores in the interior is more restricted. The internal stress assists to induce the planar orientation of the chromophores in the film and antiparallel dipole-dipole aggregation due to the electrostatic interactions between the chromophores. A possible reason is related to the rotational barrier of the chromophore. The rotational energy barrier for the rigid connection is approximately two times higher than that of the flexible connection as described in Ref. [6]. This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that under certain forces. It seems reasonable to expect that the flexible connection is much more favorable to form

subnode.title: 3.3. Internal stress by thermal contraction of linear rigid polyamide_4
subnode.metadata: chromophore size; alignment barrier; dipole aggregation; thermal contraction; orientational force; antiparallel association
subnode.content: alignment in the plane compared to the rigid connection. The third suggested reason is the fact that the chromophore DCVA is larger (about \(16\mathrm{A}\)) than the length of the repeating unit \((\approx 13\mathrm{A})\) [6]. In point of view of the structural barrier related to the size of the chromophores, alignment of DCVA molecules is more difficult than for smaller chromophores. This is because the chromophores are not necessarily phenoenoma during the cooling process is that the dipole aggregation progressively increases with increasing the dipole moment caused by more and more electron-withdrawing acceptor groups [21]. Finally this internal stress by the thermal contraction of the main-chains leads a change in the intermolecular configuration from parallel to antiparallel association of the chromophores. According to our previous work, F-DCVA polymer with flexibly linked chromophores of large size, the orientational force of the externally applied electric field could be suppressed at room temperature.

children.title: 4. Conclusion
children.metadata: polyamide; NLO chromophores; in situ corona poling; SHG measurements; internal stress; molecular structure; glass transition; electro-optic devices; main-chain motion; side-chain motion
children.content: In this study, the correlations between the macroscopic physical behavior and the microscopic molecular chemical structure have been investigated in a series of the linear rod-like polyamide with different linking structure of NLO chromophores by the in situ corona poling and SHG measurements. These polymers consist of a wholly polymeric (poly-(p-phenylene terephthalate)ide), PPTA and NLO chromophores of various sizes. Though the differently linked polymers show similar \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\)-glass transition temperatures, their physical bulk properties substantially depend on the microscopic structure. Especially in the F-DCVA polyamide with a flexibly linked chromophore of a large size, a high internal stress by a compressive force of the rigid polyamide backbones can overcome the orientational force of the externally induced electric field at room temperature. Future studies should be conducted to investigate the mechanism underlying the expected phenomenon of the internal stress and also microscopic molecular structure, which should lead to a strong correlation between the main- and side-chain motion in order to achieve a long term temporal stability required for electro-optic devices.
